extra.reasoning:
The property "globally (opted_out implies not sent)" is VIOLATED via the t_resume_glitch transition.

State: opted_out(bool), sent(bool), ticks(int 0..6). Init: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0. Property: never reach opted_out=true AND sent=true.

The only transitions that set sent:=true are t_send_allowed (guard: not opted_out — safe) and t_resume_glitch (guard: opted_out AND ticks>=6 — sets sent=true while opted_out is true). So t_resume_glitch is the dangerous path; we just need to reach opted_out=true with ticks>=6 and fire send_attempt.

Concrete 8-event counterexample (within the 10-event bound):
1. optout: t_optout (guard true) fires -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2-7. tick x6: t_tick increments ticks (capped at 6) and sets sent=false each time. After 6 ticks -> opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=6.
8. send_attempt: with opted_out=true and ticks>=6, t_resume_glitch is enabled and fires -> sent=true while opted_out remains true.

Resulting state: opted_out=true AND sent=true, directly violating opted_out implies not sent.

Note on rule interaction: on send_attempt with opted_out=true and ticks>=6, both t_send_blocked (guard opted_out) and t_resume_glitch (guard opted_out AND ticks>=6) are enabled, and no priority is specified. Under the standard nondeterministic semantics for such guarded transition systems, the reachable-state set includes the t_resume_glitch outcome, so a violating execution exists. (Even the ticks cap at 6 cooperates: ticks stays at exactly 6, keeping the glitch guard satisfied.)
